critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: The offline benchmark provides a clean comparison between the incumbent and challenger policies.
Why wrong: The objective changed between policies. The incumbent was designed for 90-day renewal probability and has been operating on its original calibration. The challenger is evaluated on net contribution margin per targeted subscriber. Comparing policies optimized for different objectives is not a model quality comparison — it is an objective-mix comparison. If the incumbent were re-calibrated and re-optimized for the margin objective, its performance might look very different.
Evidence: Re-optimize the incumbent on the same margin metric using the same training data available to the challenger. Compare incumbent and challenger at their respective best configurations on one shared frozen margin metric.

Issue 2
Claim: The doubly robust evaluation provides an unbiased estimate of the challenger's causal impact.
Why wrong: The DR nuisance models (outcome and propensity) were trained on the complete four-quarter experiment log. The Q3-Q4 reporting window is part of this log. Using the reporting period to train the estimator that evaluates the reporting period is not a holdout evaluation — it is an in-sample evaluation with extra steps.
Evidence: Re-fit nuisance models using only Q1-Q2 data and evaluate on the Q3-Q4 holdout. Compare the estimated lift to the current in-sample estimate.

Issue 3
Claim: The reported performance at the Q3-Q4 operating threshold reflects the challenger's generalizable performance.
Why wrong: The operating cutoff was chosen to maximize margin per dollar on the Q3-Q4 subscriber cohort. Reporting performance at the threshold that was optimized on the same cohort inflates the apparent outcome. This is threshold self-selection on the evaluation slice.
Evidence: Pre-declare a threshold based on earlier-quarter validation data and report challenger performance at that threshold on Q3-Q4.

Issue 4
Claim: The comparison between incumbent and challenger is symmetric in terms of optimization effort.
Why wrong: The challenger received three rounds of Bayesian hyperparameter search. The incumbent has been on its original 18-month-old calibration through two billing-system migrations. This is not a symmetric comparison of current-best policy versions.
defender_raw:
The defender addresses each issue.

Issue 1 (objective mismatch): Finance has signed off on net contribution margin as the reporting metric. This is not an arbitrary change — it is the current business owner's preferred measure of ROI that accounts for offer cost and service burden. The renewal probability metric no longer reflects economic reality after costs are netted out. Using margin is a legitimate and stakeholder-approved improvement in business alignment, not a benchmark defect.

Issue 2 (DR nuisance model training): Doubly robust estimation is specifically designed to be robust to nuisance model misspecification. Using more training data (all four quarters) to fit the nuisance models reduces variance. The causal identification comes from the randomized assignment within region-week cells, not from the nuisance model. The propensity model fit on all quarters does not contaminate the causal estimate when treatment assignment was randomized.

Issue 3 (threshold selection): Agree that selecting the threshold on the same Q3-Q4 slice and reporting on Q3-Q4 creates a self-selection issue. This is a legitimate concern. A holdout-based threshold selection would be more rigorous.

Issue 4 (asymmetric optimization): Agree that the incumbent has not been re-optimized. A symmetric comparison would require re-calibrating the incumbent on the margin objective. This is worth doing before a final commercialization decision, though the asymmetry may not fully explain the 4.5% gap.
adjudication_raw: The critic raises 4 issues. The defender partially rebuts Issue 2 (DR nuisance model training) — arguing that randomized assignment provides causal identification even when nuisance models see Q3-Q4 data. This is a reasonable point but the critic's concern is not about causal identification from the experiment — it is about whether the offline estimator's numerical estimate is inflated by in-sample fitting. The defender concedes Issues 3 and 4. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed. A clean protocol is needed: shared margin objective, nuisance models fit pre-reporting, threshold preselected, incumbent re-optimized.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: objective changed from renewal probability to net contribution margin
Issue 2: DR nuisance models fit on full experiment log including Q3-Q4 reporting period
Issue 3: operating cutoff selected on Q3-Q4 cohort used for headline reporting
Issue 4: asymmetric optimization — Bayesian tuning for challenger, frozen incumbent

empirical_test.condition: Refit nuisance models on pre-reporting data, preselect threshold, re-calibrate incumbent to margin objective, evaluate both policies on frozen holdout with block-aware uncertainty
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Challenger lift falls below zero or CI includes zero on clean holdout against re-optimized incumbent
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Challenger beats re-optimized incumbent by at least 3% incremental profit with block-aware 95% CI excluding zero
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Positive but sub-3% lift, or heterogeneous regional gains including some negatives
